headline: FUNDS LIQUIDATE INTO JUNE, CATTLE DIVERGE
subheadline: Grains shed 2% Friday as memorial money exits markets before summer doldrums.
lead: Corn closed $4.47, down 9 cents Friday and 2% for the week, as managed money booked profits ahead of the long weekend. The funds liquidated 18,000 contracts net across grain complexes Friday, classic memorial rotation out of summer-slow positions. Next week's planting data will decide if this is profit-taking or something deeper.
the_takeaway: Funds booking summer profits, not fundamentals driving grain weakness.
teaser: Memorial liquidation hits grains as cattle hold firm into grilling season.

one_number.context: Classic memorial weekend book-squaring by managed money across corn, beans, and wheat. The size tells you this wasn't fundamental selling, just funds rotating out of summer-slow positions. When funds liquidate this size ahead of quiet months, the lows usually hold until new-crop weather matters.

sections.title: Grains Close Worst May
sections.body: Corn finished $4.47, down 9 cents Friday and the worst May close since 2020. **Memorial liquidation drove the action**, not fundamentals, as managed money shed 18,000 contracts across the grain complex. December corn at $4.75 says the market's pricing normal weather through summer. The University of Nebraska wheat tour adjustments due to drought damage didn't translate to corn premium, confirming this was fund flow, not crop concern. Next week's USDA planting progress will test whether the $4.40 low holds.
sections.catalyst: Memorial Day fund liquidation ahead of summer quiet period
sections.bottom_line: Fund liquidation, not crop worry, drove worst May finish in six years.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: technical selling accelerated into outright fund exodus Friday.

sections.title: Cattle Firm Into Grilling
sections.body: Live cattle closed $239.05, down less than 1% despite broad commodity selling Friday. **Grilling season demand is offsetting the fund rotation** hitting other ags, with cutout values holding firm through the week. Feeders at $348.42 dropped harder, down 1.3%, but that's positioning for cheaper corn, not demand worry. Heat stress concerns from veterinarians remind producers that summer cattle management matters more than summer price direction. The divergence between cattle resilience and grain weakness confirms different fundamental stories.
sections.catalyst: Grilling season demand offsetting broad commodity fund rotation
sections.bottom_line: Cattle holding firm as grilling demand trumps fund liquidation hitting grains.

sections.title: Energy Extends Iran Ease
sections.body: WTI crude closed $87.36, down nearly 1% Friday as Iran tensions continued easing from March's Strait of Hormuz disruption. **The energy shock Kansas Fed President Schmid warned might not be transitory is looking increasingly transitory**. Natural gas at $3.29 reflects seasonal demand patterns more than geopolitical premium. Fertilizer prices remain the energy story farmers care about, with FTC probe confirming what producers already know: costs aren't sustainable. The energy-agriculture margin squeeze persists even as headline crude retreats.
sections.catalyst: Continued easing of Iran-Strait of Hormuz tensions from March crisis
sections.bottom_line: Iran premium fading but fertilizer costs still squeezing farm margins.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Probes Fertilizer Pricing After Farmer Complaints
outside_the_pit.body: Federal Trade Commission launched antitrust investigation into rising fertilizer costs after Iowa farmer told Chairman prices aren't sustainable. The probe confirms what balance sheets already show: input inflation is breaking farm economics faster than commodity gains can repair.

outside_the_pit.title: Nebraska Detects Tick-Borne Cattle Parasite
outside_the_pit.body: Theileria detected in several Nebraska counties, spread by Asian Longhorned Tick. Disease adds operational complexity to summer grazing just as heat stress concerns mount. Another input cost disguised as animal health.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Opens Specialty Crop Assistance June 1
outside_the_pit.body: $1.625 billion in payments for eligible producers starting Tuesday. The timing matters: June 1 is when prevent plant decisions crystallize and cash flow determines planting choices for acres still in play.

the_more_you_know.title: Memorial liquidation: the calendar's most reliable trade
the_more_you_know.body: 18,000 grain contracts liquidated Friday fits the memorial pattern perfectly. Funds book profits ahead of summer doldrums when weather matters more than position size. The trade works because June through August are statistically the quietest months for grain volatility, making large positions expensive to carry. Memorial liquidation usually marks the low until August weather scares or September harvest reality arrives.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress; corn above 85% planted confirms normal pace, below 80% adds delay premium
Weekly export sales; soybeans under 200K MT confirms China's continued absence
June weather models; first heat dome warnings would reverse fund exodus

daily_quote.text: Agriculture is our wisest pursuit, because it will in the end contribute most to real wealth, good morals, and happiness.

source_summary: CME futures, USDA, Fed officials, industry reports
